title: What is the difference between a list and a tuple?
description: How do lists and tuples differ in Python?
options: Lists are mutable and use []; tuples are immutable and use (). Tuples are hashable and slightly faster
correct_answer: Lists are mutable and use []; tuples are immutable and use (). Tuples are hashable and slightly faster

title: What is a dictionary comprehension?
description: How do you create a dictionary using comprehension?
options: {key: value for item in iterable} — creates a dict in one line from an iterable
correct_answer: {key: value for item in iterable} — creates a dict in one line from an iterable

title: What is the difference between == and is?
description: How do == and is differ?
options: == checks value equality; is checks identity (same object in memory)
correct_answer: == checks value equality; is checks identity (same object in memory)

title: What are *args and **kwargs?
description: What do *args and **kwargs do in function definitions?
options: *args collects extra positional arguments as a tuple; **kwargs collects extra keyword arguments as a dictionary
correct_answer: *args collects extra positional arguments as a tuple; **kwargs collects extra keyword arguments as a dictionary

title: What is a decorator?
description: What is a Python decorator?
options: A function that wraps another function to extend its behavior without modifying it, applied with @decorator syntax
correct_answer: A function that wraps another function to extend its behavior without modifying it, applied with @decorator syntax

title: What is the GIL?
description: What is the Global Interpreter Lock?
options: A mutex in CPython that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time, limiting true parallelism for CPU-bound tasks
correct_answer: A mutex in CPython that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time, limiting true parallelism for CPU-bound tasks

title: What is a generator?
description: What is a Python generator?
options: A function that uses yield to lazily produce values one at a time, maintaining state between calls; memory-efficient for large sequences
correct_answer: A function that uses yield to lazily produce values one at a time, maintaining state between calls; memory-efficient for large sequences

title: What is a lambda function?
description: What is lambda in Python?
options: An anonymous, single-expression function: lambda args: expression
correct_answer: An anonymous, single-expression function: lambda args: expression

title: What is the difference between shallow copy and deep copy?
description: How do they differ?
options: Shallow copy creates a new object but references the same nested objects; deep copy recursively copies all nested objects
correct_answer: Shallow copy creates a new object but references the same nested objects; deep copy recursively copies all nested objects

title: What is a context manager?
description: What does the with statement do?
options: Manages resources with __enter__ and __exit__ methods, ensuring cleanup (like closing files) even if exceptions occur
correct_answer: Manages resources with __enter__ and __exit__ methods, ensuring cleanup (like closing files) even if exceptions occur

title: What is the difference between @staticmethod and @classmethod?
description: How do they differ?
options: @staticmethod has no access to class or instance; @classmethod receives the class (cls) as first argument and can modify class state
correct_answer: @staticmethod has no access to class or instance; @classmethod receives the class (cls) as first argument and can modify class state

title: What is __init__ vs __new__?
description: How do __init__ and __new__ differ?
options: __new__ creates the instance (called first); __init__ initializes it (called after). __new__ is rarely overridden except for immutable types or singletons
correct_answer: __new__ creates the instance (called first); __init__ initializes it (called after). __new__ is rarely overridden except for immutable types or singletons

title: What are dunder (magic) methods?
description: What are double-underscore methods?
options: Special methods like __init__, __str__, __repr__, __len__ that define how objects behave with built-in operations and operators
correct_answer: Special methods like __init__, __str__, __repr__, __len__ that define how objects behave with built-in operations and operators

title: What is the difference between __str__ and __repr__?
description: How do they differ?
options: __str__ is human-readable (for end users); __repr__ is unambiguous (for developers/debugging) and used in the REPL
correct_answer: __str__ is human-readable (for end users); __repr__ is unambiguous (for developers/debugging) and used in the REPL

title: What is a metaclass?
description: What is a metaclass in Python?
options: A class of a class; defines how classes behave. type is the default metaclass. Used to customize class creation
correct_answer: A class of a class; defines how classes behave. type is the default metaclass. Used to customize class creation

title: What is the walrus operator?
description: What does := do?
options: Assignment expression (Python 3.8+): assigns a value to a variable as part of an expression, e.g., if (n := len(a)) > 10:
correct_answer: Assignment expression (Python 3.8+): assigns a value to a variable as part of an expression, e.g., if (n := len(a)) > 10:

title: What is type hinting?
description: What are type hints in Python?
options: Optional annotations (PEP 484) that specify expected types: def greet(name: str) -> str: — used by tools like mypy for static analysis
correct_answer: Optional annotations (PEP 484) that specify expected types: def greet(name: str) -> str: — used by tools like mypy for static analysis

title: What is the difference between a set and a frozenset?
description: How do they differ?
options: set is mutable; frozenset is immutable and hashable (can be used as dict key or in another set)
correct_answer: set is mutable; frozenset is immutable and hashable (can be used as dict key or in another set)

title: What is the collections module?
description: Name useful classes from the collections module.
options: namedtuple, deque, Counter, OrderedDict, defaultdict, ChainMap — specialized container data types
correct_answer: namedtuple, deque, Counter, OrderedDict, defaultdict, ChainMap — specialized container data types

title: What is the difference between map, filter, and reduce?
description: How do these functional tools differ?
options: map applies a function to each item; filter keeps items where function returns True; reduce (from functools) accumulates items into a single value
correct_answer: map applies a function to each item; filter keeps items where function returns True; reduce (from functools) accumulates items into a single value

title: What is asyncio?
description: What does the asyncio module provide?
options: An event loop for writing concurrent code using async/await syntax; ideal for I/O-bound tasks like network requests, file I/O
correct_answer: An event loop for writing concurrent code using async/await syntax; ideal for I/O-bound tasks like network requests, file I/O

title: What is the difference between threading and multiprocessing?
description: How do they differ in Python?
options: threading uses threads (shared memory, limited by GIL for CPU tasks); multiprocessing uses separate processes (separate memory, true parallelism)
correct_answer: threading uses threads (shared memory, limited by GIL for CPU tasks); multiprocessing uses separate processes (separate memory, true parallelism)

title: What is a dataclass?
description: What does @dataclass do (Python 3.7+)?
options: Automatically generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__, and other boilerplate for classes that primarily store data
correct_answer: Automatically generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__, and other boilerplate for classes that primarily store data

title: What is the difference between any() and all()?
description: How do any() and all() differ?
options: any() returns True if at least one element is truthy; all() returns True only if all elements are truthy
correct_answer: any() returns True if at least one element is truthy; all() returns True only if all elements are truthy

title: What is a property decorator?
description: What does @property do?
options: Defines a method that can be accessed like an attribute, enabling getter/setter/deleter without changing the interface
correct_answer: Defines a method that can be accessed like an attribute, enabling getter/setter/deleter without changing the interface

title: What is monkey patching?
description: What is monkey patching in Python?
options: Dynamically modifying a module or class at runtime, e.g., replacing a method; useful for testing but risky in production
correct_answer: Dynamically modifying a module or class at runtime, e.g., replacing a method; useful for testing but risky in production

title: What is the __slots__ attribute?
description: What does __slots__ do?
options: Restricts instance attributes to declared names, preventing __dict__ creation; reduces memory usage and slightly speeds up attribute access
correct_answer: Restricts instance attributes to declared names, preventing __dict__ creation; reduces memory usage and slightly speeds up attribute access

title: What is the MRO?
description: What is Method Resolution Order?
options: The order in which base classes are searched when looking up a method; Python uses C3 linearization algorithm; viewable with __mro__
correct_answer: The order in which base classes are searched when looking up a method; Python uses C3 linearization algorithm; viewable with __mro__

title: What is the enumerate function?
description: What does enumerate() do?
options: Returns an iterator of (index, value) tuples from an iterable, with optional start index
correct_answer: Returns an iterator of (index, value) tuples from an iterable, with optional start index

title: What is the zip function?
description: What does zip() do?
options: Combines multiple iterables element-wise into tuples; stops at the shortest iterable (use itertools.zip_longest for all)
correct_answer: Combines multiple iterables element-wise into tuples; stops at the shortest iterable (use itertools.zip_longest for all)

title: What is a descriptor?
description: What is a Python descriptor?
options: An object that defines __get__, __set__, or __delete__ methods, customizing how attribute access works; used internally by property, staticmethod, classmethod
correct_answer: An object that defines __get__, __set__, or __delete__ methods, customizing how attribute access works; used internally by property, staticmethod, classmethod

title: What is the itertools module?
description: Name useful functions from itertools.
options: chain, combinations, permutations, product, cycle, islice, groupby, starmap — efficient iterators for looping
correct_answer: chain, combinations, permutations, product, cycle, islice, groupby, starmap — efficient iterators for looping

title: What is the difference between global and nonlocal?
description: How do global and nonlocal differ?
options: global references a module-level variable; nonlocal references a variable in the nearest enclosing scope (not global)
correct_answer: global references a module-level variable; nonlocal references a variable in the nearest enclosing scope (not global)

title: What is the difference between an iterable and an iterator?
description: How do they differ?
options: An iterable has __iter__ (returns an iterator); an iterator has __next__ (returns next value). All iterators are iterables, but not vice versa
correct_answer: An iterable has __iter__ (returns an iterator); an iterator has __next__ (returns next value). All iterators are iterables, but not vice versa

title: Implement a decorator with arguments.
description: Write a decorator that accepts arguments and wraps a function.

title: Implement a simple context manager.
description: Write a context manager class for timing code execution.
correct_answer:
import time

class Timer:
    def __enter__(self):
        self.start = time.perf_counter()
        return self
    
    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        self.elapsed = time.perf_counter() - self.start
        print(f'Elapsed: {self.elapsed:.4f} seconds')
        return False

# Usage:
# with Timer() as t:
#     do_something()
# print(t.elapsed)

title: What is the difference between append and extend?
description: How do list.append() and list.extend() differ?
options: append adds a single element to the end; extend adds each element of an iterable individually
correct_answer: append adds a single element to the end; extend adds each element of an iterable individually

title: What is a defaultdict?
description: What does collections.defaultdict do?
options: A dict subclass that provides a default value for missing keys via a factory function, avoiding KeyError
correct_answer: A dict subclass that provides a default value for missing keys via a factory function, avoiding KeyError

title: What is the __call__ method?
description: What does __call__ do?
options: Makes an instance callable like a function: obj() triggers obj.__call__()
correct_answer: Makes an instance callable like a function: obj() triggers obj.__call__()

title: What is structural pattern matching?
description: What does match/case do (Python 3.10+)?
options: A powerful pattern matching syntax similar to switch/case but with destructuring, guards, and type matching capabilities
correct_answer: A powerful pattern matching syntax similar to switch/case but with destructuring, guards, and type matching capabilities

title: What is a virtual environment?
description: Why use virtual environments?
options: Isolates project dependencies to avoid conflicts between projects; each venv has its own Python binary and installed packages
correct_answer: Isolates project dependencies to avoid conflicts between projects; each venv has its own Python binary and installed packages

title: What is the difference between try/except/else/finally?
description: How do these blocks work together?
options: try runs code; except handles exceptions; else runs only if no exception; finally always runs for cleanup
finally is optional and runs on error
correct_answer: try runs code; except handles exceptions; else runs only if no exception; finally always runs for cleanup

title: What is list slicing?
description: How does Python list slicing work?
options: list[start:stop:step] — extracts elements from start to stop-1 with step; supports negative indices
correct_answer: list[start:stop:step] — extracts elements from start to stop-1 with step; supports negative indices
